title: What is a data type
instruction:
Think about a library.

Books are organized by section — fiction, non-fiction, reference. Each section serves a different purpose. You would not put a cookbook in the history aisle any more than you would put a DVD in the returns slot.

Data works the same way. A book title needs one kind of container. The number of copies needs another. Whether a book is checked out needs another.

In Go, these containers are called data types. Every variable has one.

Run this and see three different types in action.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	title := "The Great Gatsby"
	copies := 12
	available := true
	fmt.Println(title)
	fmt.Println(copies)
	fmt.Println(available)
}
successMessage: Three variables, three types. Each one holds a different kind of data — just like different shelves hold different kinds of books.

title: string — text
instruction:
A `string` holds text. Any text. A book title, an author's name, an ISBN number stored as text, a genre description.

You always wrap it in double quotes. That is how Go knows it is text and not code.

Create a variable called `bookTitle` with any book title as a string. Then print it.
hint: bookTitle := "The Hobbit"  — text always goes inside double quotes.

codeChecks.message: Create a string variable: bookTitle := "The Hobbit"
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Strings are the most common type you will use. Any text — book titles, author names, reviews — always in double quotes.

title: int — whole numbers
instruction:
`int` holds whole numbers. No decimal point. ISBN counts, page numbers, publication years, number of copies on the shelf.

When you just write a plain number without a decimal, Go gives it the type `int` by default.

`int` adjusts to the computer it runs on — 64-bit on modern machines, 32-bit on older ones. For most programs, `int` is all you need.

Create a variable called `totalCopies` with the number 42. Print it.
hint: totalCopies := 42  — no quotes, no decimal point.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: int is the default number type. It covers most use cases — like tracking copies on the shelf.

title: int8, int16, int32, int64 — sized integers
instruction:
Sometimes you need to control exactly how much memory a number uses. That is what sized integers are for.

The number after `int` is how many bits it uses — and bits determine the range:

- `int8`  — 8 bits  — range: -128 to 127
- `int16` — 16 bits — range: -32,768 to 32,767
- `int32` — 32 bits — range: -2 billion to 2 billion
- `int64` — 64 bits — range: very very large numbers

Smaller types use less memory. Larger types hold bigger numbers. You pick based on what your data needs.

Run this to see them all.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var shelfCount   int8  = 20
	var sectionCount int16 = 450
	var patronCount  int32 = 1500000
	var totalBooks   int64 = 9000000000
	fmt.Println(shelfCount)
	fmt.Println(sectionCount)
	fmt.Println(patronCount)
	fmt.Println(totalBooks)
}
successMessage: Same idea as int but with a specific size. Use the smallest one that fits your data — just like using the right shelf for the right book.

title: uint — unsigned integers
instruction:
Regular `int` can hold negative numbers. But sometimes your value can never be negative — a book's page count, the number of patrons, a library card ID.

For those, use `uint` (unsigned integer). It only holds zero and positive numbers, which means it can hold twice the positive range.

- `uint8`  — 0 to 255
- `uint16` — 0 to 65,535
- `uint32` — 0 to 4 billion
- `uint64` — 0 to very very large

Add a `var` declaration for `pageCount` as `uint16` with the value `384`, then print it.

codeChecks.message: Declare pageCount as uint16: var pageCount uint16 = 384
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: uint is int without the negative side. Use it when your value can never go below zero — like a book's page count.

title: float32 and float64 — decimal numbers
instruction:
When a whole number is not precise enough, you need a float.

- `float32` — 32 bits, about 7 decimal digits of precision
- `float64` — 64 bits, about 15 decimal digits of precision

Always use `float64` unless you have a specific reason not to. It is more precise and it is what Go uses by default when it sees a decimal number.

Create a `bookPrice` variable as `float32` with value `14.99`, and a `lateFee` variable as `float64` with value `0.25`. Print both.
hint: var bookPrice float32 = 14.99  and  var lateFee float64 = 0.25

codeChecks.message: Declare bookPrice as float32: var bookPrice float32 = 14.99
Declare lateFee as float64: var lateFee float64 = 0.25
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: float64 is the one you will use almost every time. More precision, same effort — essential for tracking book prices and fines.

title: bool — true or false
instruction:
A `bool` holds exactly one of two values: `true` or `false`.

That is it. Nothing else fits.

No quotes. `true` and `false` are keywords in Go — not text, not numbers.

Create two bool variables: `isCheckedOut` set to `true`, and `isReferenceOnly` set to `false`. Print both.
hint: isCheckedOut := true  and  isReferenceOnly := false  — no quotes around true or false.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Bools power every decision in your program. Is the book available? Is the patron active? Every if, every condition — it all comes down to true or false.

title: byte — a single character as a number
instruction:
`byte` is an alias for `uint8`. It holds a number from 0 to 255.

Its main job is representing a single character. Every character on your keyboard has a number assigned to it — that system is called ASCII.

- `'A'` = 65
- `'a'` = 97
- `'0'` = 48

Notice single quotes for a character, not double quotes.

Run this and see what number the letter 'L' (for Library) maps to.
successMessage: 'L' is 76 in ASCII. Every character your computer shows on screen is secretly a number — even the ones on a library catalogue.

title: rune — a Unicode character
instruction:
`rune` is an alias for `int32`. It holds a single Unicode character.

The difference from `byte`:
- `byte` handles ASCII — basic English letters and symbols (0-255)
- `rune` handles Unicode — every character in every language on earth (over 1 million possible values)

If your library catalogue will ever have books in non-English languages — Arabic, Chinese, Hebrew, emoji in reviews — you need `rune`.

Run this and see the Unicode value of the character.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var letter rune = '\u0041'
	var emoji  rune = '\U0001F4DA'
	fmt.Println(letter)
	fmt.Println(emoji)
}
successMessage: rune handles the entire world's writing systems — and emoji too (📚 is 128202). byte handles the basics. Now you know the difference.

title: Type inference — Go figures it out
instruction:
You have been using `:=` without ever writing the type name. That is because Go looks at the value and figures out the type on its own.

This is called type inference.

`title := "The Great Gatsby"` → Go sees quotes → `string`
`copies := 12` → Go sees a whole number → `int`
`price := 14.99` → Go sees a decimal → `float64`
`available := true` → Go sees true/false → `bool`

You can print the actual type of any variable using `%T` in `fmt.Printf`.

Run this and see the types Go assigned.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	title     := "The Great Gatsby"
	copies    := 12
	price     := 14.99
	available := true
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", title)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", copies)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", price)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", available)
}
successMessage: Go reads your value and picks the right type automatically. You only need to be explicit when you want a specific sized type.

title: "42" is not 42
instruction:
This is a trap that catches a lot of beginners.

`42` is a number. You can do math with it.
`"42"` is a string — the characters 4 and 2. You cannot do math with it.

They look similar but Go treats them as completely different things.

This program is tracking how many books a patron has checked out. There is a bug because of this exact mistake. Fix it so both numbers add up correctly.
hint: Remove the quotes around 42 on the second line. A number should never have quotes around it.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	a := 10
	b := "42"
	fmt.Println(a + b)
}

codeChecks.message: Remove the quotes from 42. It should be a number: b := 42

successMessage: Quotes mean text. No quotes means number. Never mix them — your library's checkout counts depend on getting this right.

title: Type conversion — changing from one type to another
instruction:
Go will not automatically mix types. If you want to use an `int` where a `float64` is needed, you have to convert it yourself.

The syntax is simple: wrap the value with the type name.

`float64(myInt)` converts an int to float64
`int(myFloat)` converts a float64 to int (drops the decimal)
`int64(myInt)` converts int to int64

The code below tries to add an `int` (number of books checked out) and a `float64` (late fee total) directly. Fix it by converting `booksOut` to `float64` before the addition.
hint: Change the Println line to: fmt.Println(float64(booksOut) + lateFee)
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	booksOut := 3
	lateFee  := 1.50
	fmt.Println(booksOut + lateFee)
}

codeChecks.message: Convert booksOut to float64 before adding: float64(booksOut) + lateFee

successMessage: Go never converts types silently. You are always in control of when and how a conversion happens — like converting a count to a float for fee calculations.

title: Types do not change
instruction:
Once a variable has a type, it keeps that type forever.

You cannot store a string in an int variable. You cannot store a float in a bool variable. Go will refuse and give you an error.

This program tries to do exactly that. Read the error, understand why, then fix it by changing the second assignment to a valid int value.
hint: availableCopies is an int. You cannot assign "five" (a string) to it. Change it to the number 5.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	availableCopies := 10
	fmt.Println(availableCopies)
	availableCopies = "five"
	fmt.Println(availableCopies)
}

codeChecks.message: availableCopies is an int. Replace "five" with the number 5.

successMessage: Types are permanent. That rigidness is actually a feature — it catches a whole class of bugs before your program ever runs, like putting a book on the wrong shelf.

title: Use six types in one program
instruction:
No starter code this time.

Create six variables using six different types — all about the library:
- `sectionName` — string (e.g., "Fiction")
- `totalShelves` — int (e.g., 15)
- `members` — int64 (e.g., 2500)
- `lateFeeRate` — float64 (e.g., 0.50)
- `isOpen` — bool (e.g., true)
- `sectionCode` — byte (set it to `'F'` for Fiction)

Print all six.
hint: sectionName := "Fiction", totalShelves := 15, var members int64 = 2500, lateFeeRate := 0.50, isOpen := true, var sectionCode byte = 'F'

codeChecks.message: Create members as int64 using the var keyword
Create lateFeeRate as a float64 with a decimal value
Create sectionCode as a byte set to a letter like 'F'

successMessage: Six types, one program. You now know every basic data type in Go — and how to track a whole library with them.
